1: Amarthi ani be-libbi lekha na' anasekha be-simha ure'eh be-tov — 'Come now, I will test you with pleasure; enjoy yourself.' The verb nasah (to test/try) introduces the pleasure experiment: the Teacher applies his empirical method to hedonism. Be-simha (in pleasure/joy) — this word simha (H8057) later becomes the Teacher's positive counsel (2:10, 26; 3:12, 22; etc.): joy in one's work is God's gift. Here the Teacher tests whether pleasure as a comprehensive life-strategy produces yitron — it does not. Ve-hineh gam-hu havel — 'but this also was futility.' The structural formula 'this also was hevel' (or 'this also is chasing the wind') marks each section of the Royal Experiment with the same verdict.
2: Le-sikhok amarthi mehulal — 'I said of laughter, “It is madness.”' Mehulal is the pual (passive intensive) participle of halal (to praise/to be foolish) — meaning 'madness' in this negative sense. The same root gives the common word hallelujah (praise the LORD): laughter here is the degenerate form of what was made for praise. Ve-la-simha mah-zoh oset — 'and of pleasure, “What does this achieve?”' The question uses oset from asah (to do/make) — what does pleasure make? The implication: pleasure is sterile, it produces nothing.
3: Tarthi be-libbi limshokh bayain et-besari — 'I searched with my heart how to cheer myself with wine.' Tarthi is from tur (to explore/scout) — the same verb as 1:13; this is systematic exploration, not mere indulgence. Ve-libbi noheig ba-hokmah — 'my heart guiding me with wisdom' — the Teacher's method is crucial: he does not abandon wisdom while exploring pleasure but uses wisdom as the guide. This is the meaning of 'testing' (nasah) pleasure: a wisdom-governed experiment, not mere hedonism. Ve-le'ehaz be-sikhlet — 'and how to take hold of folly' — taking hold of folly while maintaining wisdom is the experimental design. Ad asher ere mah-tov livnei ha'adam asher ya'asu tahat hashamayim — 'until I might see what was good for the children of man to do under heaven during the few days of their life.' Note tahat hashamayim (under heaven) rather than tahat hashemesh (under the sun) — a slight variation; 'heaven' emphasizes the dome above while 'sun' emphasizes the light source.
4: Higdalti ma'asai baniti li batim — 'I made great works: I built houses for myself.' The self-referential li ('for myself') appears repeatedly in vv. 4-8 — a deliberate accumulation: the Royal Experiment is characterized by self-oriented acquisition. Nata'ti li keramim — 'I planted vineyards for myself.' The scale of the experiment is Solomonic: 1 Kings 7 describes Solomon's building projects; the Teacher claims to have done all of it. The self-reference also sets up the verdict: 'for myself' is precisely the orientation that the Teacher discovers is futile — it produces yitron for no one when death transfers it all.
5: Asiti li ganot u-pardesim — 'I made gardens and parks for myself.' The word pardes (H6508) is a loanword from Persian (pairi-daeza = enclosed garden/park) — one of the OT's few Persian loanwords, suggesting the book reached its final form in the Persian period. In the NT, paradeisos (garden of God, Paradise) derives from this same Persian root via Greek (Luke 23:43; Rev 2:7). The Teacher's constructed pleasure garden is a shadow of what was lost in Eden and promised in the new creation. Ve-nata'ti bahem etz kol-peri — 'and planted in them all kinds of fruit trees' — the abundance of Eden (Gen 2:9: 'every tree') echoes in the Teacher's constructed garden; the human impulse to recreate paradise is precisely what the experiment tests.
6: Asiti li berekot mayim — 'I made pools from which to water the forest of growing trees.' The act of water management — pools fed from springs to irrigate an artificial forest (ya'ar) — requires comprehensive control over the environment: engineering, labor, resource. The scale of hydraulic infrastructure in the Ancient Near East was a sign of royal power (cf. Hezekiah's tunnel, 2 Kgs 20:20). The Teacher lists this achievement as evidence of his experimental scope, then negates it entirely in v.11.
7: Qaniti avadim ve-shfachot — 'I bought male and female servants.' The verb qanah (to buy/acquire) is the same word disputed at Prov 8:22 ('the LORD possessed/created me'). Here it unambiguously means commercial acquisition. The Teacher's accumulation of servants, livestock, and wealth is the comprehensive test of whether material advantage creates lasting gain. Gam-miqneh baqar va-tzon harbeh hayah li mi-kol shehayu lefanai biYerushalayim — 'I also had great herds of cattle and flocks of sheep — more than anyone before me in Jerusalem.' The superlative ('more than anyone before me') is the marker of an experiment that covers the maximum possible scope; any lesser accumulation could be dismissed as not truly testing the question.
8: Kafatsi li gam-kesef ve-zahav — 'I also gathered for myself silver and gold.' Kafatsi is from kafah — to skim/scrape together; the word emphasizes accumulation by relentless gathering. Segulat melakhim ve-hamdinot — 'the treasures of kings and provinces.' Segulah (H5459) is the covenant word for Israel as God's 'treasured possession' (Exod 19:5; Deut 7:6; Mal 3:17). Here it refers to material wealth — the Teacher has accumulated the nations' treasures as an experiment. The word's covenant resonance (Israel as God's segulah) creates ironic contrast: the Teacher collects a segulah of material goods, which will prove hevel; the divine segulah of a redeemed people is what cannot be declared vanity.
9: Ve-gadalthi ve-hosafti mi-kol asher-hayah lefanai biYerushalayim — 'So I became greater than all who were before me in Jerusalem.' Gadal (to become great) and yasaf (to add/increase) describe compound growth. Af-hokmati amdah li — 'and my wisdom also remained with me.' This is the key methodological claim: the Teacher did not abandon wisdom during the pleasure experiment. He did not become a hedonist; he maintained his capacity for evaluation. The experiment's design requires this: only someone who retains wisdom can accurately evaluate whether pleasure produces yitron.
10: Ve-khol asher sha'alu einai lo atzalthi mehem — 'And whatever my eyes desired I did not withhold from them.' The verb atzal (to withhold) is the same root used of Abraham who 'did not withhold' his son Isaac (Gen 22:12, lo chatsakta). The Teacher's self-indulgence is total: nothing the eye desired was refused. Ki-libbi samach mi-kol amali ve-zeh hayah helqi mi-kol amali — 'for my heart found pleasure in all my toil — and this was my reward for all my toil.' Helqi (H2506) — my portion/share — is a key word in Ecclesiastes' positive counsel. The Teacher discovers that joy in the process of work is itself the reward: not the product accumulated, but the experience of working with pleasure is the helqi, the portion that actually belongs to one.
11: Ve-faniti ani be-khol-ma'asai sheasu yadai — 'Then I considered all that my hands had done.' The verb panah (to turn/face) describes a deliberate reorientation of attention: the Teacher turns to evaluate what the accumulation experiment has produced. Ve-hineh hakol havel ure'ut ruach — 'all was futility and chasing the wind.' Re'ut ruach — the MKT renders this 'chasing the wind'; the word re'ut (from ra'ah, to feed/shepherd) might mean 'feeding on wind' — consuming what has no substance. Ve-ein yitron tahat hashemesh — 'there was nothing to be gained under the sun.' The exact phrase from 1:3 (mah yitron) closes this subsection: the experiment yields the predicted result. The repetition is structural, not literary laziness.
12: Ve-faniti ani lir'ot hokmah ve-holalot ve-sikhlet — 'So I turned to consider wisdom, madness, and folly.' The Teacher pivots from the Royal Experiment to comparative reflection. Ki mah ha'adam sheyavo ahar hamelekh — 'For what can the man who comes after the king do?' The question has been read as: (a) no one can replicate the royal experiment at this scale, or (b) the successor king (3:16-4:16 takes up this motif) faces the same vanity. Et asher-kvar ashuhu — 'only what has already been done' — the heir inherits the fruits without the process, and can add nothing beyond what the royal accumulator has already done. This connects to the 'no new thing' (1:9) motif.
13: Ve-ra'iti ani she-yesh yitron la-hokmah min-ha-sikhlet — 'Then I saw that wisdom excels folly.' The word yitron (advantage/profit) here describes wisdom's superiority over folly — but notice the context: wisdom has yitron, but this yitron is not the same as the ultimate yitron under the sun that the book is seeking. Wisdom is genuinely better than folly; the Teacher does not deny this. Ke-yitron ha'or min-ha-choshekh — 'as light excels darkness.' The comparison (ke-yitron = 'as the advantage of') is an analogy: wisdom over folly is like light over darkness. But as vv. 14-16 will show, this advantage does not survive the one event that equalizes both wise and foolish: death.
14: He-chakham einav be-rosho ve-ha-kesil ba-choshekh holekh — 'The wise person has his eyes in his head, but the fool walks in darkness.' The idiom 'eyes in his head' means attentiveness and perceptive awareness — the wise person sees where he is going. The fool (kesil, H3684 = the dull/stubborn fool, one who acts against known wisdom) walks in the dark. Ve-yadati gam-ani she-miqreh echad yiqreh et-kulam — 'Yet I also perceived that the same event happens to all of them.' The word miqreh (H4745) — 'event/fate/lot' — is key to Ecclesiastes' anthropology. It occurs 7 times (2:14, 15; 3:19 [x3]; 9:2, 3) and always describes the common death that erases all distinctions. Wisdom's advantage (eyes in the head) does not alter miqreh.
15: Ve-amarthi ani be-libbi ke-miqreh ha-kesil gam-ani yiqreh li — 'What happens to the fool will happen to me also.' The Teacher's logic is unsentimental: if both the wise and the foolish die the same death, what is the practical yitron of wisdom in the long run? La-mah chakhamti ani az yoter — 'So why was I so very wise?' Yoter (H3148) means 'exceedingly/more/exceeding' — the superlative wisdom experiment was pointless if death erases the advantage. Ve-dibarti be-libbi she-gam-zeh havel — 'and I said in my heart that this also is futility.' The self-address formula again; the Teacher's internal monologue is the structure of the experiment's evaluation.
16: Ki ein zikaron le-chakham im-ha-kesil le'olam — 'For there is no lasting remembrance of the wise any more than of the fool.' The word zikaron (memorial/remembrance) carries the weight of the covenant memorial system: what YHWH 'remembers' is sustained; what has no zikaron passes from existence as if it had never been. The Teacher's point: both wise and foolish leave no permanent human memorial; future generations will not distinguish them. Ve-eikh yamut he-chakham im-ha-kesil — 'How does the wise man die? Just like the fool.' The parallel death equalizes all human achievement. The Teacher is not denying that wisdom is better (v.13); he is denying that wisdom achieves the immortality of the memorial that would constitute yitron.
17: Ve-sane'ti et-ha-chayyim — 'So I hated life.' The verb sane' (to hate) is strong: the Teacher has moved from frustration to something like existential revulsion. This is the emotional nadir of the Royal Experiment — not a philosophical conclusion but a felt response to the futility of all he has accumulated and observed. Ki ra alay hama'aseh sheassoh tahat hashemesh — 'because what is done under the sun seemed evil to me.' Ra (evil/bad) is the Teacher's evaluative word for what the futility-saturated world presents. Ki hakol havel ure'ut ruach — 'for all is futility and chasing the wind.' The full verdict formula closes the subsection. The Teacher's 'hatred of life' is not suicidal — it is the honest recognition that life 'under the sun,' without eschatological frame, is insufficient ground for sustained joy.
18: Ve-sane'ti ani et-kol amali sheani amel tahat hashemesh — 'And I hated all my toil in which I had labored under the sun.' The word amel (H5998, to labor/toil) gives the noun amal (H5999) — used 35 times in Ecclesiastes; it means both the activity of laboring and the product of that labor. She-ani amel tahat hashemesh — 'in which I had labored under the sun' — the repetition of the laboring verb emphasizes the investment of self. She-eni'chennu la'adam sheyihyeh acharai — 'because I must leave it to the man who comes after me.' The problem of inheritance: the accumulator cannot control what the heir does with what is inherited. The wealth passes, but not the wisdom that produced it. This is one of the book's most humanly piercing observations about death and its consequences.
19: U-mi yode'a ha-yih-yeh chakham o sakal — 'And who knows whether he will be wise or foolish?' The phrase mi yode'a ('who knows?') is a characteristic Ecclesiastes interrogative marking epistemological uncertainty (cf. 3:21; 6:12; 8:1). The heir might be a wise steward of the inheritance or a fool who dissipates it — the accumulator cannot know. Vi-yishlet be-khol amali — 'yet he will have control over all the fruit of my toil' — the verb shalat (to have dominion/control) echoes Gen 1:28 (radah/dominion); the heir exercises dominion over what the accumulator's wisdom produced. Gam-zeh havel — 'this also is futility.' The brevity of the conclusion matches the bitterness of the insight: a single phrase marks one of the human condition's most frustrating realities.
20: Ve-savothi ani le-ya'esh libbi — 'So I turned about and gave my heart up to despair.' Savothi (from savav, to go around/turn) recalls the circular motions of 1:6 (the wind whirling), now applied to the Teacher himself: he turns, as everything turns, in a cycle that goes nowhere. Ya'esh (despair/hopelessness) — the noun is used only twice in the OT (here and Job 17:11). Al-kol ha-amal sheamalti tahat hashemesh — 'over all the toil at which I had labored under the sun.' The preposition al ('over') with despair: the Teacher's despair is directed specifically at the accumulated labor, not at life generically. His despair is as precise as his analysis.
21: Ki yesh adam she-amalo be-hokmah uve-da'at uve-kishron — 'Because sometimes a person who labored with wisdom and knowledge and skill.' The triple characterization — wisdom, knowledge, skill (kishron, H3788, efficiency/advantage) — describes the ideal worker. Ve-le'adam shelo amal-bo yitnennu helqo — 'must leave everything to someone who did not work for it at all.' Helqo (his portion) — the concept of helq reappears: the heir receives a helq he did not earn, while the accumulator's portion (helq) that he earned through wisdom goes with him to death. Gam-zeh havel vera'ah rabbah — 'this also is futility and great evil.' The formula intensifies: not just havel but vera'ah rabbah (great evil) — the unjust distribution of the fruits of wisdom is the worst category the Teacher has yet assigned.
22: Ki mah-hoveh la'adam be-khol amalo — 'For what does a man get from all his toil?' The word hoveh (H1933) — 'what comes to' or 'what befalls' — is an unusual form; some manuscripts read yih-yeh (what will be). The question is the same as 1:3 (mah yitron) but sharpened: not 'what lasting gain' but 'what actually arrives/comes to the man?' U-vera'yon libbi shehu amel tahat hashemesh — 'and striving of heart with which he labors under the sun.' Ra'yon libbi (striving of his heart) — the word ra'yon is related to re'ut ruach (chasing the wind): the inner striving and the outer metaphor are from the same root.
23: Ki khol-yamav makhavim ve-ka'as inyano — 'For all his days are full of sorrow, and his work is grief.' Makhavim (sorrows/pains) — plural of makhov (H4341, grief/pain), same word as 1:18: the Teacher returns to his earlier observation. Inyan again: the 'business' of life is grief. Gam-ba-layla lo-shakhav libboh — 'even at night his heart does not rest.' The heart (lev) is the organ of thought and evaluation in Hebrew psychology; it does not stop working even when the body rests. Gam-zeh havel hu — 'this also is futility.' The relentlessness of sorrow — no rest even at night — is itself characterized as hevel.
24: Ein tov ba'adam sheyokhal ve-shata ve-hera'ah et-nafsho be-amalo — 'There is nothing better for a person than to eat and drink and find enjoyment in his toil.' The phrase ein tov ('there is nothing better') introduces the carpe diem counsel that recurs throughout Ecclesiastes (3:12, 22; 5:18; 8:15; 9:7-9). The MKT's 'find enjoyment in his toil' renders hera'ah et-nafsho — literally, 'to cause his soul/life to see good' — the nefesh (H5315, soul/life/self) finding its experience of the good. Gam-zoh ra'iti ani ki mi-yad ha'Elohim hi — 'This also, I saw, is from the hand of God.' The pivot: the carpe diem counsel is not mere hedonism but a recognition that the capacity for enjoyment is itself a divine gift (mi-yad ha'Elohim, 'from God's hand' — the hand that provides).
25: Ki mi yokhal ve-mi yachush chutz mimmenni — 'For apart from God, who can eat or find enjoyment?' The MT reads mimmenni ('apart from me') — a first-person form — but the LXX reads mimmennu ('apart from him,' i.e., God). Most English versions follow the LXX here. The MT reading (the Teacher speaking in his own voice) would mean: 'who can enjoy apart from what I have experienced?' — a claim that the experiment was comprehensive. Either reading supports the theological point: enjoyment is not self-generated; it requires a giver (v.24: 'from the hand of God') or a framework (the Teacher's wisdom). The verse is brief — a single rhetorical question — where the point is made entirely by the question's force.
26: Ki le-adam she-tov lefanav natan hokmah ve-da'at ve-simha — 'For God gives wisdom, knowledge, and joy to the one who pleases him.' The triple gift (wisdom, knowledge, joy) to the one who 'is good before him' (tov lefanav) — the idiom 'good before God' means morally acceptable, pleasing to God's sight. Ve-la-chote natan inyan le'esof ule-khnas — 'but to the sinner he assigns the task of gathering and accumulating.' The sinner's inyan (business) is le'esof ule-khnas (to gather and to accumulate) — both verbs describe hoarding. Litet le-tov lifnei ha'Elohim — 'only to hand it over to the one who pleases God.' The ironic reversal: the sinner labors to accumulate, then transfers it to the God-pleaser at death. Gam-zeh havel ure'ut ruach — the full verdict formula closes the chapter.
